Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Hi everyone, I'm Tom, and two days ago I wrecked my voice.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I'll try my best to be comprehensible today, but as always, if you're having trouble understanding my spoken words,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: feel free to turn on subtitles, those usually have the full script I use in there and should be pretty accurate.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But anyways, today's topic is going to be heated beds that has been one of the most requested topics,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: so today I'm going to tell you all about them.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So heated beds have one very obvious main purpose,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and that is to get the surface of the print bed up to temperature where the print will stick to it better

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and as such will not come loose from the unavoidable warping forces that the layer by layer printing process

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: generates. But heated bed has a second side effect that can help reduce warping at the source,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and that's because the radiated heat will keep the print warm. You see that the largest factor in

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: how much a print is going to warp is roughly speaking, the difference between the plastics glass

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: transition temperature and the cooled down temperature during a print. So either you have PLA with a

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: low glass transition temperature of 50 degrees Celsius, and let it cool down to 20 degrees with an

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: unheated bed, or you use ABS with a glass transition temperature of about 100 degrees Celsius,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and just make sure it doesn't cool down as far.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: The extreme case of this would be a heated chamber which keeps your ABS prints at 60 to 80 degrees Celsius,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: so you'd end up with the same 30 degree temperature difference.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Heat chambers or at least enclosed printers are extremely nice for the high 10 plastics,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but that's an entirely different topic. Now because of those two effects, I still haven't found a

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: plastic that is not easier to print with a heated bed. I mean sure you can print PLA on blue tape and

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: some of the soft plastics onto glass on such a room temperature, but even for PLA having the option

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: of heating the bed surface and printing straight to glass is just so much more convenient than having

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: to pry your prints for blue tape. Of course heated beds are absolutely necessary for some plastics

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: like ABS which can be printed onto a cold surface, but that involves a whole new level of clutches.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: As far as the heated beds themselves go, there are two basic types that you can get. One is the

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: classic PCB heater originally designed by Joseph Prusha, which was the first heated bed that you could

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: buy. Along with the Prusha mantle, it basically established the mid-size form factor of a 200 by

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: 200 millimeter build platform. You can still get heater PCBs that are very close to the original

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: design. Some of them can now be switched from 12 to 24 volts. Others have different form factors

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: like the around-advanced intended for delta printers, but there's two issues I personally have with

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: heater PCBs and those are one. It can be pretty hard to get them to make good contact with whatever

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: sheet material you put in them, which becomes necessary because, too, they have a tendency to warp

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and polish each, especially the cheaper and thinner ones. As the control applies power to the bed,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the side with the heating tracks heats up much faster than the opposite side. PCB expands on

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the hotter side and in the process of that, bows the entire pin surface. In fact, one of my first big

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: mysteries with my back then Prusha II was that the second layer just wouldn't stick onto the

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: first one. And as it turned out, because I was reducing the heated bed temperature for all layers,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but the first one, the heated bed would shut off for a while until it reached that cooler temperature

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and the guested bowed away from the nozzle and basically left a huge gap between the first and second

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: layer. So the other option, which I like a lot more, or heater mats, that you can stick onto the bottom

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: of your print sheet, in my case aluminum because I also use an inductive probe for bed auto adjustment.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You can get these heated from a few resellers in Europe or the Americas, but you can also get them

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: directly from the guys that make them at any size, voltage and power rating. For example, I had this one

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: made as a 150 millimeter square with 150 watts, which will take it up to 150 degrees Celsius.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: More than enough for even printing polycarbonate. I also have a 300 millimeter round bed incoming that

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: is made to run at 230 volts mains power since shoving 600 watts through a power supply is kind of

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: pointless and it's pretty hard to wire everything properly at those currents anyways. But please,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: if you are using mains power anywhere, be careful, make sure everything is unplugged before you

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: start working on the wiring and ground your print the frame through the protective earth rail.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I've heard from a couple folks that the silicon heater mats tend to give up after a while,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but that you haven't had any problem with mine so far. I've been torturing it quite a bit.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: If you're wondering what kind of power you need for a certain size, it's relatively simple.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: You need at least about 0.6 watts for every square centimeter of heated area to get to 120 degrees

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: for ABS or about 0.2 watts for that same area to get to about 60 degrees for PLA.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But if you want to save some time during heat-up, you can also go up to 1 watt per square centimeter.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Just make sure your electronics in handling. Most electronics are limited to 10 or 15 amps of

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: output current, which means that they can just about power a pressure PCB heater at 12 volts

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: or something a bit more powerful at 24. With respect to your board and make sure none of the

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: transistors or connect this burn-up when you're heating it up for the first time. Now, as far as sheet

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: material for heated beds goes, again, there are two main choices. You can either use glass and clip

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that to a heater PCB or use aluminum in the same fashion or directly stick a heater mat to it.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: I prefer aluminum, cheap, it won't break ever, it will distribute the heat from the heater bed

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: extremely evenly, and it will ensure that the surface temperature is exactly what you want it to be.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And you can directly use it with an inductive sensor. For smaller beds, flatness doesn't matter

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: as much, but for larger ones you can get mild, plain aluminum sheets at almost any size and thickness.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: This guy, for example, will end up on top of that aforementioned 300 millimeter heater mat in the service

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: board, which I hope I'll get to build over Christmas, but it looks like the ballins for it aren't going

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: to arrive on time. Anyways, the other material you can use is glass sheet and that is pretty much

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: always perfectly flat due to the way it's made. Many people use borosilicate glass, which is

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: similar to pyrexane made to resist temperature shocks, but I found that regular 3 millimeter

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: window glass works just as well. Even when I heated up one side of my double heated bed to 120 degrees

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and left the other side cold, it never flinched. Two millimeters is too thin and has a huge risk of

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: breaking just from the forces, it sometimes takes to pry a print off, so I wouldn't recommend using glass

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: that thin. Also, if you're planning on using a heater mat instead of a PCB, I definitely recommend

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the heater mats are handmade and can be quite irregular in their heat output, which aluminum

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: helps smooth out, but also if you stuck it to a glass sheet and break that, well you can probably

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: trash the heater too. On top of whatever sheet material you may choose to use, you can use different

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: toppings as well, but for many plastics you don't have to. Many uses print directly onto clean,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and this is important, absolutely clean glass, made plastics will directly stick to that.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Then there's captain and PET tape, which are nice for ABS and other higher temp plastics,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: and then there is the glue section of glue stick, diluted pv8 glue and hairspray,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: each of those having their own unique properties, and as always, what might work for everybody else

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: might just not work for you, so there's definitely some trial and error involved, but there's always

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: some way it works out. Most of the surface choices lose their stickiness when they cool down, so

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: prints are going to be much easier to remove, or pop right off if you wait for the bed to cool down

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: after a print has finished. One last thing you want to talk about is thermistors in heated beds,

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: or for heated beds, because they are always somewhat awkward to place if you want a good reading.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: If you tape one to the bottom of the heated bed, the top might end up being much cooler than the

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: spot you're actually measuring. If you put it on top of the heated bed, you obviously have the risk

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: of running into it with your printer's nozzle. Either way, you should really make sure that it is

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: well insulated from the air around it, and gets good contact with the spot you're trying to measure.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Some neuro PCBs have a spot in the center to place a thermistor, which is actually a really decent

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: place to put it. Silicon heaters on the other hand can be configured with a thermistor built in,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: but I found that it reports temperatures that are way too low. Either way, if you have a thermistor

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: in a spot that isn't exactly where you need to read the temperature, I'd at least recommend waiting

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: a bit after heating up before you start to print, that way the heat has some time to soak through

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the entire construction you have on there. Or if you have an aluminum bed, you can also use the

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: E3DV6 method and clamp a thermistor into the aluminum plate. That way you'll get exactly the

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: temperature the bed actually is it. So I guess that's got to do it for today. If you have any questions

Text: left, please feel free to leave a comment and I'll try to answer those. I'll see you next week

Text: until then. I wish you all a politically correct, happy holidays. I don't care much about what you
